projects.project:
Project 1 – Cardboard Robot Buddy
(Creativity & Parts of a Robot)

classes.title: What is a Robot? – Exploration & Discussion
classes.description: Watch a 5-min video of real robots (toys, factory arms, vacuum robots). Class discussion: What can a robot do? Each student draws their dream robot buddy and labels 3 parts (head, arms, body). Gallery walk: share designs with the class.
classes.materials: Projector/tablet, drawing sheets, color pencils, 'Parts of a Robot' handout

classes.title: Design My Robot Buddy – Sketch & Plan
classes.description: Using a template, students plan their cardboard robot: size, shape of head/body/arms, decoration ideas. Teacher demonstrates how to trace and cut cardboard shapes safely. Students select materials and trace body parts onto cardboard.
classes.materials: A3 cardboard sheets, pencils, scissors (rounded tip), robot design template worksheet

classes.title: Build It! – Assemble the Robot
classes.description: Cut pre-traced cardboard parts and assemble body using paper fasteners for movable joints (arms that swing). Glue on googly eyes, add paper details, color and decorate. Each robot must have at least one moving part.
classes.materials: Pre-traced cardboard parts, paper fasteners, googly eyes, glue sticks, color markers, stickers

classes.title: Robot Show & Tell – Showcase Day
classes.description: Students place robots on display desks. Each stands next to their robot and tells the class: 'My robot is called ___. It can ___. I made it from ___.' Class votes for 'Most Creative' and 'Most Friendly Robot'. Reflection card completed.
classes.materials: Completed robots, name tags, voting slips, reflection cards

projects.project:
Project 2 – Traffic Light Model
(Colours, Sequencing & Simple Circuits)

classes.title: Traffic Rules & Light Sequences – Circle Time
classes.description: Teacher shows photos/video of traffic signals. Students play a colour-signal game: teacher holds red/yellow/green cards, students act (stop/ready/go). Discussion: What would happen if there were no traffic lights? Worksheet: colour and sequence the traffic light.
classes.materials: Coloured cards (red/yellow/green), traffic light photos, worksheet, projector

classes.title: My First Circuit – LED Glow Experiment
classes.description: Teacher demonstrates how a coin cell battery, wire, and LED work together. Students (in pairs) touch wire ends to LED legs on the battery — LED glows! They test: What happens if you disconnect one wire? Introduce terms: circuit, open, closed, current.
classes.materials: Coin cell batteries (CR2032), LEDs (red, yellow, green), copper wire offcuts, insulating tape

classes.title: Build the Traffic Light – Hands-on Assembly
classes.description: Students construct a cardboard traffic light tower (pre-cut box). Poke holes for 3 LEDs. Connect each LED to a coin cell using copper tape strips. Test each light individually. Label the colours. Add 'STOP/READY/GO' labels beside each light.
classes.materials: Cardboard box (pre-cut), copper tape, 3 LEDs (R/Y/G), 3 coin cells, tape, markers

projects.project:
Project 3 – Paper Circuit Greeting Card
(Simple Circuits + Creativity)

classes.title: Conductors & Insulators – Material Test Lab
classes.description: Students test materials (foil, pencil line, rubber, cardboard, wire) by connecting them between battery and LED. Sort results into 'conductor' and 'insulator' chart. Introduction to copper tape as a flat conductor. Draw and record findings.
classes.materials: Coin cell, LED, 2 pieces of wire with clips, materials kit: foil, eraser, plastic, cardboard strip, pencil-marked paper

classes.title: Design Your Card – Plan the Circuit Path
classes.description: Students fold A4 card paper to create a greeting card. On the back panel, plan the circuit path using pencil: where the battery tab goes, where the copper tape runs, where the LED peeks through. Teacher models on a demo card.
classes.materials: A4 card paper (folded), pencils, 'Circuit Path Planner' worksheet, sample demo card

classes.title: Build & Light Up Your Card
classes.description: Students lay copper tape along their planned path, carefully turning corners (fold the tape, do not cut). Place LED legs on tape, cover with small piece of tape. Slide coin cell under a folded flap. Press — light glows! Decorate the front with a design.
classes.materials: A4 card, copper tape, LEDs, coin cells, decorating supplies, markers, stickers, binder clip

classes.title: Gift the Card – Celebration & Reflection
classes.description: Students write a message inside and share their glowing card with a classmate or teacher. Discussion: What made the light work? What would happen if we break the tape path? Students fill a 1-page reflection: 'My circuit worked because ___.'
classes.materials: Completed cards, reflection sheet, envelopes (optional)

projects.project:
Project 4 – Windmill Power Toy
(Energy, Motion & Simple Machines)

classes.title: Wind Energy Exploration – How Windmills Work
classes.description: Short video: wind farms and traditional windmills. Teacher spins a paper pinwheel in front of a fan — students observe. Discussion: What makes the blades spin? What is the spin used for? Students make a simple paper pinwheel (fold-and-pin instructions).
classes.materials: Projector, small desk fan, paper (15×15 cm), pins, pencil with eraser, Pinwheel Template sheet

classes.title: Build the Windmill Stand – Cardboard Engineering
classes.description: Students construct a stable cardboard tower (H-shape or A-frame) using the slot-and-tab method (no glue needed for main joints). Decorate with landscape drawings (hills, sun). Test stability: can it stand alone? Reinforce weak spots with tape.
classes.materials: Pre-scored A4 cardboard (slot-tab template), scissors, markers, ruler

classes.title: Add Motor & Blades – Make It Spin!
classes.description: Teacher demonstrates: DC motor connected to battery makes shaft spin. Students attach paper blades (4-blade propeller, pre-cut) to motor shaft. Mount motor on tower top. Connect 2 AA battery pack. Hold near fan or blow — blades spin! Observe energy conversion.
classes.materials: DC motor (3V), 4-blade paper propeller, 2×AA battery holder, wires, electrical tape, blue-tack

projects.project:
Project 5 – My Smart House Model
(Systems Thinking & Design)

classes.title: What is a Smart Home? – Systems Exploration
classes.description: Teacher shows images of regular house vs. smart home (auto lights, sensor fan, smart lock). Discussion: What does each room need? Students fill a 'Smart Home Design Sheet': draw each room and list one smart feature per room.
classes.materials: Projector, Smart Home Design A3 worksheet, color pencils

classes.title: Build the House Structure – Cardboard Architecture
classes.description: Students construct a 2-room house using folded cardboard. Walls, a divider, and a simple pitched roof. Pre-cut pieces are provided; students assemble using glue tabs. Add windows (rectangular cut-outs) and a door flap that opens.
classes.materials: Pre-cut cardboard house kit (walls, roof, divider), glue sticks, scissors, markers

classes.title: Add the LED Light – Circuit Inside a House
classes.description: Students install one LED light in the 'bedroom' using copper tape running along the wall from a coin cell battery. A bent cardboard flap acts as the switch (pressing it completes the circuit). Test: press the switch → light glows!
classes.materials: Copper tape, LED, coin cell, small cardboard switch flap, tape

classes.title: House Tour & Storytelling – Showcase
classes.description: Students give a 1-minute 'house tour': 'This is the bedroom. The light turns on when I press here.' They draw one additional smart feature they wish their house had. Class discusses: What technology could make homes safer for everyone?
classes.materials: Completed house models, 'My Wish Feature' drawing sheet

projects.project:
Project 6 – Pattern Art using Shapes
(Logic, Math & Visual Patterns)

classes.title: What is a Pattern? – Explore & Identify
classes.description: Show patterns in real life: fabric, tiles, animal stripes, wallpaper. Students clap along to AB rhythm patterns. Sort shape cards into AB, ABB, ABC pattern sequences on a desk mat. Identify the rule for each pattern and create a new one.
classes.materials: Pattern cards (shapes), desk mat, projector, real-life pattern images, rhythm sticks

classes.title: Number & Skip Counting Patterns
classes.description: Students fill pattern strips: 2, 4, __, 8, __ and 5, 10, __, 20. Use coloured cubes to visualise skip counting. Create a colour pattern on a 10-grid strip using 2 colours. Connect to early multiplication concept (groups of 2).
classes.materials: Number pattern strips, coloured counting cubes, 10-grid strips, color pencils

classes.title: Create Pattern Artwork – Geometry + Art
classes.description: Students use shape stencils (triangle, circle, square, hexagon) to design a repeating tile pattern on A3 paper. Must use at least 2 shapes and 3 colours in a repeating rule. Frame and display the artwork on the classroom wall.
classes.materials: Shape stencils, A3 paper, color pencils/markers, ruler, sample pattern tiles

projects.project:
Project 7 – Maze Board Game
(Problem Solving & Logical Thinking)

classes.title: Solve a Maze – Introduction to Paths & Decisions
classes.description: Students receive 3 mazes (easy → hard). Discuss: What is a dead end? What is the shortest path? Strategy: always try one way, backtrack if stuck. Introduce graph thinking: junctions and paths. Race: who finishes all 3 mazes first?
classes.materials: Printed maze worksheets (3 difficulty levels), pencils, stopwatch

classes.title: Design Your Own Maze – Graph Paper Planning
classes.description: Students design a maze on graph paper (15×15 grid). Rules: must have a START and FINISH; at least 3 dead ends; one correct path only. Plan in pencil first, then trace in pen. Swap with a partner: can they solve it?
classes.materials: Graph paper (15×15 grid), pencils, pens/markers, ruler

classes.title: Build the Cardboard Maze
classes.description: Transfer the graph paper design to a cardboard base. Use cardboard strips (2 cm wide) glued vertically as maze walls. Allow to dry. Paint or colour the paths. Add a marble start zone and finish flag.
classes.materials: A3 cardboard base, pre-cut cardboard strips, PVA glue, paint/markers, marble, small flag sticker

classes.title: Maze Tournament – Play & Compete
classes.description: Students race marbles through each other's mazes. Timed challenge: who completes 3 mazes the fastest? Vote for 'Most Tricky' and 'Most Beautiful' maze. Reflect: How is solving a maze like solving a real problem?
classes.materials: Completed maze boards, marbles, stopwatch, voting slips, reflection cards

projects.project:
Project 8 – Simple Pulley Lifter
(Simple Machines & Force)

classes.title: Simple Machines All Around Us – Introduction
classes.description: Show and discuss all 6 simple machines with real objects or pictures (lever, wheel, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, screw). Focus on pulley: how does it change the direction of force? Demo: teacher lifts a bag with a rope over a dowel rod. Students feel the difference.
classes.materials: Real-object kit, rope, dowel rod, small bag with weight, projector

classes.title: Test a Pulley – Science Experiment
classes.description: Students measure force needed to lift 100g weight directly (using spring balance) vs. using a single fixed pulley. Record results in a table. Question: does a pulley make lifting easier, or just change direction? Discuss load vs. effort.
classes.materials: Spring balances, 100g weights, pulley wheel, rope, stand/tripod, data recording sheet

classes.title: Build the Pulley Lifter – Hands-on Construction
classes.description: Students build a cardboard A-frame stand. Thread rope through a pulley wheel fixed at the top. Attach a small paper cup (load) to one rope end. Pull the other end down to lift the cup. Load the cup with small objects. How much can it lift?
classes.materials: Pulley wheel, rope, A-frame cardboard stand, paper cup, paper clips, small weights (erasers/coins)

projects.project:
Project 9 – Animal Robot Craft
(Biomimicry & Inspired Design)

classes.title: Biomimicry – How Nature Inspires Machines
classes.description: Show inspiring examples: Velcro from burrs, Shinkansen nose from kingfisher beak, drone from dragonfly. Students choose one animal and list: 'My animal is ___. It moves by ___. A machine inspired by it is ___.' Sketch the animal and the inspired machine.
classes.materials: Projector, Biomimicry Match card game, inspiration cards, sketch worksheet

classes.title: Build the Animal Body – Frame Construction
classes.description: Select an animal (cat, dog, bird, fish, crab) and receive a basic template. Cut and assemble body frame from cardboard/foam. Add structural details (wings with straws, legs with pipe cleaners, tail with wire). Focus on stable structure.
classes.materials: Cardboard/foam sheets, pipe cleaners, straws, wire (soft), scissors, glue, googly eyes, craft feathers

classes.title: Add Movement – Motor-Powered Animation
classes.description: Attach a small craft motor (3V) to one moving part: tail wag, wing flap, or wheel spin. Connect to 2×AA battery with a simple on/off switch. Observe the movement. Refine by adjusting the part position. Present: 'My animal robot can ___.'
classes.materials: Craft motors (3V), 2×AA battery holder, wire, small switch or alligator clips, tape

projects.project:
Project 10 – My First Machine Book
(Reflection, Portfolio & Showcase)

classes.title: Year in Review – What Did We Build?
classes.description: Teacher displays photos of all 9 projects on the board. Students fill a reflection grid: draw a small sketch of each project and write 3 words describing what they built. Discussion: Which project was hardest? Which was your favourite? What did you learn?
classes.materials: Printed/projected photos of all projects, A3 reflection grid worksheet, color pencils

classes.title: Make My Machine Book – Design & Illustrate
classes.description: Students receive a pre-stapled A5 booklet (10 pages). Each page is for one project: draw the project, write the name, complete the sentence 'I learnt that ___.' Cover page: 'My Machine Book by [Name]'. Illustrate with colours.
classes.materials: Pre-stapled A5 booklets, color pencils, markers, project photo handouts for reference

classes.title: Showcase Day – Exhibition & Celebration
classes.description: All models and books displayed on desks. Other class or parents invited for a 20-minute walk-through. Students stand at their station and explain their favourite project. Hand out 'Young Engineer' certificates.
classes.materials: All project models, machine books, display labels, certificates, parent invitation cards

projects.project:
Project 1 – Torch Light Circuit
(Electricity Basics & Circuit Building)

classes.title: What is Electricity? – Safe Exploration
classes.description: Discuss: Where do we use electricity? (lights, fans, phones). Introduce battery as stored energy. Show a torch and open it — students see battery, bulb, switch. Explore: what connects them? Introduce schematic symbols: battery (⊥), bulb (●), switch (/).
classes.materials: Torch (opened), battery, bulb, projector, Circuit Symbols poster, worksheet

classes.title: Build a Simple Circuit – First Glow!
classes.description: Students connect a battery, wire, and bulb on a circuit board mat. Use crocodile clips for safe connections. Test: does it glow? Remove one wire — does it still glow? Introduce: open vs. closed circuit. Add a paper-clip switch to control the bulb.
classes.materials: D-cell batteries, torch bulbs, wire with crocodile clips, paper-clip switch, circuit mat

classes.title: Build the Torch Light – Housing & Switch
classes.description: Students roll cardboard into a cylinder (torch body). Fit battery inside. Thread wire out the end. Attach bulb at the front in a cardboard cap. Add a paper-clip switch on the side. Decorate and test the working torch by pointing it at the wall in a darkened corner.
classes.materials: Cardboard rolls, D-cell battery, torch bulb, crocodile wire, paper-clip, tape, markers

projects.project:
Project 2 – Buzz Wire Game
(Precision, Conductors & Control)

classes.title: Conductors & the Science of Touch
classes.description: Recap: conductors and insulators. Introduce the buzz wire game concept: a wire loop must travel along a bent wire path without touching — touching completes the circuit and buzzes. Watch a demo video. Plan: what components are needed? Students draw the circuit diagram.
classes.materials: Demo buzz wire game (teacher-made), projector, components identification worksheet

classes.title: Bend the Wire Path – Engineering the Game
classes.description: Students (in pairs) bend a thick wire into a curvy path (3 bends minimum) and mount both ends into a cardboard/foam base. Ensure the wire is stable and upright. This becomes the 'course' for the loop.
classes.materials: Thick galvanised wire (pre-cut 60 cm), pliers (teacher handles), cardboard/foam base, tape

classes.title: Wire the Circuit & Make It Buzz
classes.description: Students thread a smaller loop of wire around the path wire. Connect: one wire from battery(+) to path wire, other wire from battery(–) to buzzer, buzzer's other lead to the loop handle wire. Touch → buzz! Practice keeping the loop off the path.
classes.materials: 9V battery, buzzer, thin insulated wire, alligator clips, loop handle (cardboard grip)

classes.title: Buzz Wire Tournament
classes.description: Run a timed buzz wire challenge — who can complete the path with fewest buzzes? Discuss: how does a steady hand relate to precision in engineering? Reflection: 'Conductors help us build games because ___.'
classes.materials: Completed buzz wire games, stopwatch, score sheet, reflection cards

projects.project:
Project 3 – Smart Fan Model
(Motors, Rotation & Energy)

classes.title: Spinning Things – How Motors Work
classes.description: Open a small DC motor: coil, magnets, rotation. Show: touch wires to battery → shaft spins; swap wires → spins the other way. Students observe and note the direction. What household appliances use motors? (Fan, blender, washing machine.) Sketch and label a motor.
classes.materials: DC motor, 2×AA battery, wires, projector, Motor Spotting worksheet

classes.title: Make Fan Blades – Aerodynamics Play
classes.description: Students cut 4 rectangular blades from card (8×3 cm each). Fold a 1 cm flap at one end and glue to a cardboard disc hub at 90° intervals. Angle the blades slightly (30°). Which blade angle moves the most air? Test near a tissue paper strip.
classes.materials: Card paper, scissors, cardboard disc (hub), protractor, glue, tissue paper for testing

classes.title: Assemble the Smart Fan
classes.description: Students build a cardboard fan stand (tower with motor mount at top). Glue motor into mount. Attach blade assembly to motor shaft. Wire motor to 2×AA battery pack with a push-button switch. Fan ON! Discuss speed vs. battery voltage.
classes.materials: DC motor, fan blade assembly, cardboard tower, 2×AA battery holder, push-button switch, wires, tape

projects.project:
Project 4 – Moving Car Model
(Wheels, Axles & Motion Transfer)

classes.title: Wheels, Axles & How Cars Move
classes.description: Explore: why are wheels round? Roll a circle vs. a square — which goes farther? Push a toy car and observe axle spin. Introduce: wheel, axle, chassis. Students draw a labelled diagram of a car side view with all key parts.
classes.materials: Toy car (opened), square block vs. round wheel demo, worksheet for labelling

classes.title: Build the Chassis – Car Structure
classes.description: Students assemble a cardboard chassis. Cut the base plate. Insert 2 bamboo skewer axles through straw tunnels (glued to chassis underside). Push bottle-cap wheels onto axle ends. Test: does it roll straight? Adjust wheel alignment.
classes.materials: Cardboard base, 2 bamboo skewers, 4 plastic straws, 4 bottle caps, tape, scissors

classes.title: Add the Motor & Test Drive
classes.description: Mount a DC motor at the rear. Connect motor shaft to rear axle with a short rubber tube coupling. Wire to battery pack with a switch. Car rolls forward! Test on a slight ramp. Troubleshoot wobbly axles and friction issues.
classes.materials: DC motor, rubber tube coupling, 2×AA battery holder, switch, wires, tape, ramp board

classes.title: Design & Race Challenge
classes.description: Students decorate their car (paint, stickers). Run a distance race on a flat floor — whose car goes farthest on one battery charge? Measure with tape. Discuss: What causes friction? How could we make it faster? Record results.
classes.materials: Completed cars, tape measure, starting ramp (optional), decorating supplies, result sheet

projects.project:
Project 5 – Scratch Jr Story Animation
(Block Coding & Sequencing)

classes.title: What is Coding? – Unplugged Introduction
classes.description: Act out a 'human robot' game: teacher gives verbal instructions ('move 3 steps forward, turn right, wave'). Students follow. Discuss: a computer follows instructions exactly. Introduce Scratch Jr blocks as picture-instructions. Demo on screen.
classes.materials: Tablets/PCs with Scratch Jr, printed block reference card, open floor space for unplugged activity

classes.title: Explore Scratch Jr – Characters, Backgrounds & Motion Blocks
classes.description: Students log into Scratch Jr. Choose a character (sprite) and background. Use motion blocks: move right, move left, jump. Add a Start (green flag) block at the beginning. Task: make the character walk from one side of the screen to the other and back.
classes.materials: Tablets/PCs, Scratch Jr installed/web, step-by-step instruction sheet with screenshots

classes.title: Add Looks & Sound – Create a Mini Story
classes.description: Students add a second character. Use 'say' blocks (speech bubble) and 'play sound' blocks. Create a 3-scene mini story: character enters → says hello → makes a sound. Use 'wait' blocks between events for timing. Students share with a neighbour.
classes.materials: Tablets/PCs, Scratch Jr, story storyboard planning sheet

classes.title: Share & Showcase – Class Screening
classes.description: Each student's animation is projected. Class watches 2-minute screenings. Peer feedback: 'I liked ___' and 'One suggestion is ___'. Students save/export projects. Discuss: what would you add next time?
classes.materials: Tablets/PCs, projector for sharing, peer feedback form

projects.project:
Project 6 – Bridge Building Challenge
(Structures, Load & Forces)

classes.title: How Bridges Work – Engineering Principles
classes.description: Show images of arch, suspension, beam, and truss bridges. Discuss: what forces act on a bridge? (compression, tension.) Demo: push two ends of a foam piece together vs. pull apart. Students sort bridge images by type and label the forces.
classes.materials: Projector, foam piece, Bridge Types sorting cards, worksheet

classes.title: Design Your Bridge – Engineering Drawing
classes.description: Constraints: span 30 cm gap, use only ice-cream sticks and PVA glue, max 30 sticks. Students sketch a design: top view, side view. Plan triangular supports (strongest shape). Get teacher approval before building.
classes.materials: Graph paper, pencil, Bridge Design Template worksheet, ruler

classes.title: Build & Load-Test the Bridge
classes.description: Students build their bridge (allow glue to dry with clips). Load test: place 20g weights one at a time. Record: maximum load before collapse. Compare: which design was strongest? Why? Analyse: where did it break first?
classes.materials: Ice-cream sticks, PVA glue, clothespins (to hold while drying), 20g weight set, data sheet

projects.project:
Project 7 – Shape-Based 3D Model
(Spatial Thinking & 3D Forms)

classes.title: From 2D to 3D – Understanding 3D Shapes
classes.description: Identify 3D shapes in the classroom (cube, cylinder, sphere, cone, cuboid). Use nets (flat fold patterns) — show how a square net folds into a cube. Students match net cards to 3D shape cards. Discuss faces, edges, vertices.
classes.materials: 3D shape props, net cards (flat shapes that fold), 3D shape matching game

classes.title: Model with Clay/Foam – Hands-on 3D Creation
classes.description: Students build a small 3D scene: a house (cuboid body + pyramid roof), a tree (cylinder trunk + sphere leaves), a sun (sphere). Use clay or foam. Connect shapes with toothpicks. Focus on proportion and stability.
classes.materials: Air-dry clay or foam pieces, toothpicks, base board, shape reference poster

classes.title: Name, Count & Display
classes.description: Students count faces, edges, and vertices of each shape they made. Fill in a 3D shapes data table. Write a label card for the model: name of scene, shapes used, number of each. Place on the display shelf for a gallery.
classes.materials: Completed models, label cards, data table worksheet, display shelf

projects.project:
Project 8 – Automatic Door Model
(Mechanisms & Motion)

classes.title: Automatic Doors – How Do They Know?
classes.description: Show a video of an automatic sliding door at a supermarket. Discuss: What makes it open? (Sensor detects person.) What mechanism moves the door? (Motor and belt/pulley.) Students label a door diagram: sensor, motor, door panel, track.
classes.materials: Projector, Automatic Door Parts labelling sheet

classes.title: Build the Door Frame & Track
classes.description: Students construct a cardboard door frame with top and bottom tracks from cardboard strips. The door panel is a cardboard sheet that slides in the tracks. Add a guide channel. Test: door slides smoothly with a gentle push.
classes.materials: Cardboard frame, door panel, strip tracks, scissors, glue, tape, ruler

classes.title: Motorise the Door
classes.description: Thread a long loop of thread through a pulley at the top of the frame. Attach one side of the loop to the door panel. Other side winds onto a DC motor spool. Motor turns → door opens/closes. Wire with switch. Demonstrate to the class.
classes.materials: DC motor, cardboard spool, thread loop, pulley, 2×AA battery, switch, alligator clips

projects.project:
Project 9 – Sound-Activated Toy
(Sound Waves & Sensors)

classes.title: The Science of Sound – Vibrations & Waves
classes.description: Pluck a rubber band on a ruler — students see vibration and hear sound. Sprinkle sugar on a drum skin and bang — watch it jump. Discuss: sound is vibration travelling through air. Introduce microphone module: it converts sound vibrations to electrical signal.
classes.materials: Ruler + rubber band, drum with sugar/salt, small speaker, microphone module (demo only)

classes.title: Design the Sound-Activated Toy
classes.description: Students design a toy: when you clap, an LED flashes OR a motor jiggles. Plan on a design sheet: what happens at clap, what component reacts, how it is wired. Teacher guides which design is feasible with available components.
classes.materials: Design planning worksheet, component photos for reference

classes.title: Build & Test the Sound Toy
classes.description: Wire microphone sound module output to an NPN transistor base. Transistor switches the LED or small motor in the collector circuit. Connect to 3V battery. Clap loudly → LED flashes. Troubleshoot connections. Celebrate with a clap!
classes.materials: Microphone sound module, NPN transistor (BC547), LED/small motor, 3V battery, wires, breadboard

projects.project:
Project 10 – Mini Science Fair
(Research, Build & Present)

classes.title: Choose Your Topic – Problem Identification
classes.description: Students pick from 5 pre-approved topic cards: 'Does a fan cool water?', 'Which bridge shape is strongest?', 'Which material conducts best?', 'Can a magnet move a car?', 'Which blade angle spins fastest?' Define: question, hypothesis, materials needed.
classes.materials: Topic cards (5 options), hypothesis writing frame, parent permission note

classes.title: Experiment & Build
classes.description: Students conduct their experiment or build their model. Record 3 observations in a results table. Take photos. Draw a diagram. Write a conclusion: 'My hypothesis was ___. The result was ___. This is because ___.'
classes.materials: Materials vary by topic, results table worksheet, camera/tablet for photos

classes.title: Science Fair Day – Present to Judges
classes.description: Display boards set up in the hall. Students stand next to their display and present to visiting judges (senior teachers). 2-minute verbal presentation. Answer 1 question from the judge. Class votes for favourite project.
classes.materials: Display boards, completed models/results, voting slips, certificates

projects.project:
Project 1 – Motor-Powered Robot
(DC Motors, Actuation & Assembly)

classes.title: Introduction to Motors & Actuation
classes.description: Discuss: what is an actuator? (Something that creates movement.) Identify actuators in real life: motor in fan, piston in car engine, servo in robotic arm. Open a DC motor — identify coil, brush, shaft. Students record observations in an engineering notebook.
classes.materials: DC motor (opened), projector, engineering notebook, Types of Actuators reference sheet

classes.title: Design the Robot – Plan Before Building
classes.description: Students sketch the robot on an A4 design sheet: how many wheels? Where is the motor? How is the chassis shaped? Use design thinking steps: Define, Ideate, Prototype. Get teacher feedback on feasibility before building.
classes.materials: A4 design sheet, pencils, Design Thinking Steps poster

classes.title: Build & Wire the Motor Robot
classes.description: Students assemble chassis (cardboard), mount motor on rear axle. Connect motor shaft to rear wheels. Wire motor to AA battery pack and slide switch. First drive! Troubleshoot: wheel alignment, motor mount looseness, wiring polarity.
classes.materials: Cardboard chassis kit, DC motor, rubber coupling, 2×AA battery, slide switch, wires, tape

classes.title: Test, Improve & Showcase
classes.description: Mark a 1-metre track with tape. Students race their robots. Measure speed (distance/time). Identify one improvement to make (better wheels, lower friction). Implement the fix. Final reflection: What did engineering mean for you today?
classes.materials: Completed robots, tape measure, stopwatch, reflection cards

projects.project:
Project 2 – Traffic Police Signal
(Control Logic & LED Sequencing)

classes.title: Control Logic – Sequencing with Rules
classes.description: Introduce control logic: 'IF it is red → STOP. IF it is green → GO.' Play a timing game: teacher is the controller, students act as traffic. Map the sequence on a flowchart. Extend: how would a timer control lights without a human?
classes.materials: Projector, flowchart template worksheet, traffic role-play space

classes.title: Build the Signal Post
classes.description: Students build a 3-tier traffic light post using cardboard tubes and a base. Three holes punched for LEDs. Wire each LED to a breadboard in a row. Test each LED individually using a 3V power rail. Label R, Y, G.
classes.materials: Cardboard tube post, breadboard, 3 LEDs (R/Y/G), resistors (330Ω), 3V battery, wires

classes.title: Program the Sequence – Manual Switch Board
classes.description: Use a manual switch board (3 separate switches, one per LED) to cycle through R→Y→G sequences. Students manually practice the sequence timing: 'Red ON 3 seconds, Yellow ON 1 second, Green ON 3 seconds.' Then demonstrate the choreographed sequence to the class.
classes.materials: Manual switch board (3 switches), completed traffic light model, stopwatch for timing

projects.project:
Project 3 – Scratch Animated Game
(Events, Loops & Game Logic)

classes.title: Introduction to Scratch – Stage, Sprites & Events
classes.description: Open Scratch 3.0. Explore: Stage (background), Sprite (character), Script area (blocks). Add a sprite and backdrop. Drag 'when green flag clicked' and 'move 10 steps' — run it! Extend: add 'repeat 10' loop. Discuss: what is a loop?
classes.materials: Computers/tablets with Scratch 3.0, step-by-step reference card, projector for teacher demo

classes.title: Design the Game – Characters & Rules
classes.description: Students plan a simple catching/avoiding game on a planning sheet: Sprite 1 (player, controlled by arrow keys), Sprite 2 (falling object), Goal (catch it or avoid it). Define the winning condition. Sketch the game screen layout.
classes.materials: Game design planning sheet, pencils, Scratch 3.0

classes.title: Build the Game – Coding Mechanics
classes.description: Code Sprite 1: 'When left/right key pressed → move'. Code Sprite 2: 'When green flag clicked → go to random x/y → glide to bottom → repeat'. Add a score variable: 'If Sprite 1 touches Sprite 2 → change score by 1'. Test and debug.
classes.materials: Computers with Scratch 3.0, step-by-step coding guide

classes.title: Polish, Play & Peer Review
classes.description: Students add: sound effects, animated backgrounds, game over message when score = 10. Play each other's games. Peer review sheet: 'The game is fun because ___, I suggest ___.' Teacher selects 2 games to project and discuss the code structure.
classes.materials: Scratch 3.0, peer review worksheet, projector

projects.project:
Project 4 – Smart Dustbin
(IR Sensors & Simple Automation)

classes.title: What Makes a Device Smart? – Sensor Introduction
classes.description: Show: smart dustbin video (lid opens automatically). Discuss: what triggers it? (Proximity sensor.) Introduce IR sensor: emits infrared light, detects reflection. Demo: wave hand in front of IR module → LED changes state. Students sketch the trigger–action flow.
classes.materials: Projector, IR sensor module, LED, 3V battery for demo, Trigger-Action worksheet

classes.title: Wire the IR Sensor Circuit
classes.description: Students wire: IR sensor VCC→5V, GND→GND, OUT→LED anode (via 330Ω resistor). When object detected → LED ON. Familiarise with breadboard rows and columns. Explore: adjust sensitivity potentiometer and observe detection range change.
classes.materials: IR sensor module, breadboard, LED, 330Ω resistor, 5V USB power or Arduino 5V rail, jumper wires

classes.title: Build the Dustbin & Add the Servo Lid
classes.description: Construct dustbin body from a plastic bottle/box. Mount servo motor at lid hinge. Wire servo to a motor driver. When IR detects hand → servo moves 90° (lid opens) → hold 2 sec → lid closes. Build, test, and refine.
classes.materials: Plastic bottle/cardboard box, servo motor, servo horn, cardboard lid, linkage wire, 5V supply

projects.project:
Project 5 – Water Level Indicator
(Probes, Conductivity & Alerts)

classes.title: How Do We Detect Water Level?
classes.description: Experiment: dip two bare wires into salt water — does an LED glow? (Yes! Salt water conducts.) Try distilled water — different result. Introduce: conductivity-based sensing. Show a tank with 3 wire pairs at Low/Medium/High positions.
classes.materials: Salt water, distilled water, 2 bare wire ends, 3V battery, LED, worksheet for recording results

classes.title: Design the Water Level Circuit
classes.description: Multi-LED indicator: 3 probes at Low/Mid/High. Each probe pair connected to a transistor that switches an LED. Students draw the schematic on circuit paper. Teacher checks designs before building.
classes.materials: Circuit design worksheet with schematic symbols, pencils, component list

classes.title: Build & Test the Water Level Indicator
classes.description: Students build the circuit on a breadboard. Mount probes inside a clear plastic bottle (tank). Fill water to each level and verify correct LED activates. Optional: replace top LED with a buzzer for overflow alert.
classes.materials: Breadboard, 3 NPN transistors, 3 LEDs (green/yellow/red), resistors, bare wire probes, plastic bottle tank, 5V supply

projects.project:
Project 6 – Earthquake-Safe Building
(Structural Engineering)

classes.title: Forces on Buildings – Earthquake Science
classes.description: Show video: building collapse in earthquake. Discuss: horizontal shaking stress. Demonstrate: push a cardboard stack sideways vs. a braced triangle structure. Why is a triangle the strongest shape? Students test cubes vs. triangulated frames with a gentle shake.
classes.materials: Projector, cardboard stack, triangulated cardboard frame, shake-test base, worksheet

classes.title: Design the Earthquake-Safe Structure
classes.description: Constraints: build using blocks, rubber bands, and cardboard. Must be at least 30 cm tall. Must support a 200g weight on top. Must survive a 10-second shake test. Students sketch design with triangular bracing details.
classes.materials: Design sheet, pencils, ruler, block/rubber band component list

classes.title: Build & Shake-Test the Structure
classes.description: Students build their structure within 20 minutes. Place 200g weight on top. Teacher shakes the base board steadily for 10 seconds. Did it survive? Measure height. Rank: height × survival = score. Rebuild/improve if time allows.
classes.materials: Blocks, rubber bands, cardboard strips, glue, 200g weight, base board, ruler, scoring sheet

projects.project:
Project 7 – 3D House Design in Tinkercad
(CAD Introduction & Spatial Thinking)

classes.title: What is CAD? – Introduction to 3D Design
classes.description: Show real-world CAD outputs: phone case design → 3D printed case; building blueprint → actual building. Introduce Tinkercad interface: workplane, shape library, zoom, rotate. Students complete a guided tutorial: place a box, resize it, group two shapes.
classes.materials: Computers with Tinkercad (tinkercad.com), projector for demo, Tinkercad Quick-Start guide

classes.title: Design Your House – Walls, Roof & Windows
classes.description: Task: design a house with 4 rooms. Use box shapes for walls and roof. Use cylinder/box combinations for chimney. Hollow the inside using the 'hole' feature (subtractive modelling). Add window openings. Label each room using the text tool.
classes.materials: Tinkercad, House Design Checklist (walls/roof/windows/rooms), projector

classes.title: Refine & Add Details – Doors, Garden, Garage
classes.description: Students add details: a door arch (cylinder cut-out), a garden (green flat box), a car in the driveway. Adjust colours. Export a screenshot. Discuss: how would this file be sent to a 3D printer? View a time-lapse of 3D printing.
classes.materials: Tinkercad, 3D printing video clip, screenshot export worksheet

classes.title: Gallery Walk – Peer Feedback on 3D Designs
classes.description: Rotate between computers (or project each design for 1 min). Provide 1 star and 1 wish feedback using sticky notes. Teacher selects the most detailed design for a virtual 3D print demo shown to the class.
classes.materials: Computers, printed screenshot gallery, sticky note feedback

projects.project:
Project 8 – Smart Alarm System
(PIR Sensors & Safety Logic)

classes.title: How Security Systems Work – Sensor Logic
classes.description: Discuss: where are security alarms used? (Homes, banks, museums.) Show: PIR sensor detects movement by reading body heat infrared. Demo: wave hand past PIR → LED on teacher's board lights up. Discuss: false alarms — what causes them?
classes.materials: Projector, PIR module demo board, LED, battery, Smart Alarm Design worksheet

classes.title: Wire the PIR Alarm on a Breadboard
classes.description: Students wire: PIR→VCC/GND/OUT → transistor base → buzzer and LED in collector circuit. Power: 5V. Test: walk past sensor → buzzer and LED activate. Troubleshoot: PIR sensitivity, re-trigger delay. Students adjust the sensitivity knob.
classes.materials: PIR sensor module, NPN transistor, buzzer, LED, resistors, breadboard, 5V supply, jumper wires

classes.title: Mount & Demonstrate the Alarm System
classes.description: Mount PIR sensor on a cardboard wall panel of a model room. Hide wires neatly. Mount buzzer and LED as alarm indicators. Students take turns: one person walks in → alarm triggers. Add a cardboard armed/disarmed switch. Showcase to class.
classes.materials: Completed alarm circuit, cardboard room model, mounting tape, switch, wires

projects.project:
Project 9 – Maze-Solving Algorithm Game
(Computational Thinking & Logic)

classes.title: Algorithms in Real Life – Step-by-Step Thinking
classes.description: Discuss: what is an algorithm? Give examples: recipe, morning routine, GPS directions. Activity: write step-by-step instructions to guide a blindfolded classmate from door to a target (using only: Forward N steps, Turn Left, Turn Right). Execute and debug.
classes.materials: Open floor space, blindfold, instruction sheet, coloured tape for target

classes.title: Design a Solvable Maze on Paper
classes.description: Students design a maze on 1 cm graph paper (20×20 grid). Rules: single correct path; at least 5 decision junctions; exactly 1 START and 1 END. Swap with a partner to verify it is solvable. Trace the solution path in pencil.
classes.materials: 1 cm graph paper, pencils, ruler, Maze Design Checklist

classes.title: Build the Physical Maze & Write the Algorithm
classes.description: Transfer maze to cardboard base with cardboard strip walls. Then write the solution as a movement algorithm (e.g., F3, L, F2, R, F4 — forward N, left, right). Swap mazes and try to execute each other's algorithm on the physical maze. Debug if wrong.
classes.materials: A3 cardboard base, cardboard strips, PVA glue, algorithm writing sheet, pencil

projects.project:
Project 10 – Innovation Expo
(Design Thinking, Build & Present)

classes.title: Innovation Thinking – Identify a Problem to Solve
classes.description: Show innovator profiles (young inventors who solved real problems). Students brainstorm 5 problems in their school/home environment. Use design thinking: 'Who has the problem? What do they need? What could technology do?' Select one problem to solve.
classes.materials: Projector, design thinking worksheet, sticky notes for brainstorming

classes.title: Prototype Your Solution
classes.description: Students build a rough working prototype using any available materials (electronics, cardboard, code). Teacher circulates and guides: 'Does it address the problem? Can someone use it easily?' Focus: it must work, not look perfect.
classes.materials: Mixed materials (cardboard, electronics kit, Scratch on computers), timer (35 min build), checklist

classes.title: Innovation Expo – Present to Peers & Judges
classes.description: Students create a poster (A3) with: Problem, Solution, Materials, How It Works, What I Would Improve. Display in the classroom. Rotating judges (peers + teachers) give 1–5 star ratings on innovation, function, and presentation. Awards given.
classes.materials: Completed prototypes, A3 poster, judging rubric, star rating stickers, awards certificates

projects.project:
Project 1 – Line Follower Robot
(Arduino + IR Sensors + Motor Driver)

classes.title: Intro to Arduino – Hardware & First Sketch
classes.description: Introduce Arduino Uno: microcontroller, digital/analog pins, USB power. Open mBlock (visual block coding). Run first sketch: Blink LED. Students drag blocks: Setup → set LED pin output; Loop → LED HIGH, wait 1s, LOW, wait 1s. Upload → LED blinks!
classes.materials: Arduino Uno, USB cable, PC with mBlock, LED, 220Ω resistor, breadboard, jumper wires

classes.title: IR Sensors – Detect a Black Line
classes.description: Mount 2 IR sensor modules below a test chassis. Test: sensor over white surface → output HIGH; over black tape → LOW. Map sensor states to motor actions: both white → go straight; left on black → turn left; right on black → turn right. Record in a truth table.
classes.materials: 2 IR sensor modules, breadboard, 5V supply, black tape track (test strip), serial monitor worksheet

classes.title: Wire Motor Driver & Build the Chassis
classes.description: Introduce L298N motor driver: controls 2 DC motors from Arduino signals. Wire: Arduino → L298N inputs; L298N outputs → motors; 7.4V battery → L298N. Build cardboard chassis with motor mounts and caster wheel. Assemble IR sensors at front.
classes.materials: L298N motor driver, 2 DC motors, wheels, 7.4V battery, cardboard chassis, caster wheel

classes.title: Program, Test & Tune the Line Follower
classes.description: Students write/upload line following logic in mBlock. Place robot on a black tape oval track. Does it stay on track? Adjust sensor height and sensitivity pot. Tune: if overshooting curves, reduce motor speed PWM value. Time 3 laps.
classes.materials: Completed line follower robot, oval black tape track, laptop, tuning worksheet, timer

projects.project:
Project 2 – Automatic Night Lamp
(LDR + Arduino + Threshold Logic)

classes.title: LDR – How Light Controls a Circuit
classes.description: Show LDR: in bright light, resistance drops; in dark, resistance rises. Voltage divider demo: LDR + 10kΩ resistor, measure output on multimeter as you cover the LDR. Introduce: analog pin, analogRead() returns 0–1023. Students record readings at 3 light levels.
classes.materials: LDR, 10kΩ resistor, multimeter, 5V supply, LDR Voltage Divider worksheet

classes.title: Read LDR Value & Serial Monitor Debug
classes.description: Students build LDR voltage divider on breadboard, connect to Arduino A0. Program: read A0 every 500ms → print to Serial Monitor. Cover the LDR with hand — watch value change. Set a threshold (e.g. <300 = dark). Print DARK or BRIGHT.
classes.materials: Arduino, LDR, 10kΩ resistor, breadboard, USB cable, laptop with mBlock

classes.title: Add the LED & Build the Lamp Housing
classes.description: Extend code: If value < threshold → LED pin HIGH (lamp ON); else → LED OFF. Connect white LED via 220Ω resistor to Arduino pin 9. Test in dark vs. bright room. Mount circuit in a cardboard lamp housing with a window for LDR.
classes.materials: Arduino, LED (white), 220Ω resistor, cardboard lamp housing, tape, paint optional

projects.project:
Project 3 – Smart Parking System
(Ultrasonic Sensor + LED Indicators)

classes.title: Ultrasonic Sensors – Measuring Distance with Sound
classes.description: Explain echolocation (bats, dolphins). HC-SR04 emits 40kHz pulse (TRIG), measures echo return time (ECHO), calculate: distance = (time × 340 m/s) / 2. Demo: bring hand closer → Serial Monitor shows decreasing distance reading.
classes.materials: HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor, Arduino, breadboard, laptop, serial monitor distance table

classes.title: Design the Parking Lot Model
classes.description: Students design a 3-bay parking lot from cardboard. Each bay: one ultrasonic sensor pointing inward. Map logic: distance > 20 cm → space empty (green LED); distance < 20 cm → occupied (red LED). Draw the wiring plan on paper before building.
classes.materials: A3 cardboard, HC-SR04 sensors, 3 green/3 red LEDs, resistors, parking lot template

classes.title: Program & Demonstrate the Parking System
classes.description: Wire sensors to Arduino. Program if/else logic for each bay. Green LEDs light for empty spaces, red for occupied. Place toy cars → system correctly shows availability. Add a count display: 'X spaces free' printed to serial monitor. Demo to class.
classes.materials: Arduino, LED set, cardboard parking model, toy cars, USB cable, serial monitor

projects.project:
Project 4 – Obstacle Avoidance Robot
(Ultrasonic + Motor Driver + Logic)

classes.title: Obstacle Avoidance Logic – Decision Trees
classes.description: Draw decision tree: If distance < 20 cm → STOP, turn right; else → go forward. Program on paper first (pseudocode). Discuss: how does a Roomba vacuum avoid walls? Show robot navigation video. Students write pseudocode for their own avoidance strategy.
classes.materials: Projector, pseudocode worksheet, obstacle avoidance navigation video

classes.title: Build & Wire the Obstacle Avoider
classes.description: Mount HC-SR04 at the front of the robot chassis. Wire to Arduino along with L298N motor driver. Connect 7.4V battery. Confirm: distance reading and motor control both work independently before combining.
classes.materials: Robot chassis (reuse or new kit), HC-SR04, L298N, 7.4V battery, wires

classes.title: Upload Code & Navigate an Obstacle Course
classes.description: Upload full obstacle avoidance code. Set up a mini obstacle course (cardboard boxes). Run robot — does it avoid obstacles? Fine-tune: adjust distance threshold, turning duration. Students race robots through the course. Log debugging steps taken.
classes.materials: Completed robot, cardboard obstacle course, laptop for upload, timer, Debugging Log sheet

projects.project:
Project 5 – Digital Weather Station
(DHT11 Sensor + LCD Display)

classes.title: Weather Data & Sensors – What We Measure
classes.description: Discuss: what data makes up weather? (Temp, humidity, pressure, wind.) Show DHT11 sensor: measures temperature AND humidity on one pin. Demo: breathe on sensor → humidity rises on Serial Monitor. Introduce I2C LCD display and how it communicates.
classes.materials: DHT11 sensor, Arduino, laptop, LCD I2C display for demo, projector

classes.title: Wire DHT11 & Read Data to Serial Monitor
classes.description: Students wire: DHT11 DATA pin → Arduino D2 with 10kΩ pull-up resistor. Install DHT library. Run sample sketch: read temperature (°C) and humidity (%) → print to Serial Monitor every 2 seconds. Explore: fan blowing → temperature reading drops slightly.
classes.materials: DHT11, Arduino, 10kΩ resistor, USB cable, laptop with DHT library installed

classes.title: Add LCD Display & Build the Weather Station Box
classes.description: Wire I2C LCD to Arduino (SDA→A4, SCL→A5). Program: show Temp: XX°C on line 1, Humidity: XX% on line 2. Build a cardboard weather station box with sensor window and LCD display window. Label it. Compare readings with a real thermometer.
classes.materials: I2C LCD 16×2, I2C module, jumper wires, cardboard weather station box, glue, markers

projects.project:
Project 6 – Scratch Game with Score & Timer
(Variables, Conditions & Game Design)

classes.title: Scratch Variables & Conditions – Level Up
classes.description: Recap Scratch basics. Introduce: Variables (create a score and timer variable). Use change score by 1 block on collision. Use timer sensing block to count down. Code: When timer > 30 → stop all → show Game Over. Demo on projector.
classes.materials: Computers with Scratch 3.0, projector, Variables and Conditions reference sheet

classes.title: Design Your Game – Game Design Document
classes.description: Students complete a mini GDD: Title, Genre (catch/avoid/platform), 2 Sprites, Win/Lose condition, Score tracking. Sketch 3 screen states: Start screen, Gameplay screen, Game Over screen. Review with a classmate before coding.
classes.materials: GDD template worksheet, pencils

classes.title: Code the Full Game
classes.description: Students build their game in Scratch: start screen with instructions, animated player (keyboard controlled), randomly moving target sprite, score counter, 60-second countdown timer, game over message. Use clone blocks for multiple targets.
classes.materials: Computers with Scratch 3.0, coding guide for clones and timer

classes.title: Play, Peer Test & Iterate
classes.description: Students swap computers and play each other's games for 3 minutes. Write one bug report and one compliment. Fix the reported bug. Present the improved game to the teacher. Discuss: what makes a game fun vs. frustrating?
classes.materials: Scratch 3.0, peer test feedback form, projector for final showcase

projects.project:
Project 7 – Personalised 3D Keychain
(Tinkercad Advanced + 3D Print Process)

classes.title: Tinkercad – Advanced Shapes & Subtractive Design
classes.description: Review Tinkercad basics. Introduce: Hole feature (subtractive design). Demo: create a rectangular plate, place a heart-shaped hole on it → combine → heart-cutout pendant. Students replicate the demo step-by-step then explore other hole shapes.
classes.materials: Computers with Tinkercad, step-by-step demo guide, projector

classes.title: Design Your Keychain
classes.description: Students design a personalised keychain: must include their name (using Tinkercad text tool), one symbol (star, heart, shield), and a hole for the keyring (cylinder hole at top). Dimensions: max 6 cm × 3 cm × 0.5 cm thick.
classes.materials: Tinkercad, Keychain Design Checklist, size ruler reference on screen

classes.title: Export & Understand 3D Printing
classes.description: Export STL file. Teacher shows slicing software (Cura) with the students' file: layers, infill, support. Discuss: print time, material (PLA). If printer available, print during class. If not, show time-lapse of similar keychain printing.
classes.materials: Tinkercad, Cura slicer (teacher demo), 3D printer or time-lapse video, STL export worksheet

projects.project:
Project 8 – Wireless Doorbell System
(Push Button + Buzzer + Custom Tones)

classes.title: Digital Input – How Push Buttons Work
classes.description: Introduce: digital input pin on Arduino. Demo: wire momentary push button with 10kΩ pull-down resistor to Arduino D2. Code: If digitalRead(D2)==HIGH → print PRESSED. Students wire and test. Discuss: debouncing — why does it sometimes register multiple presses?
classes.materials: Arduino, push button, 10kΩ resistor, jumper wires, laptop, serial monitor

classes.title: Add the Buzzer & Play Tones
classes.description: Wire passive buzzer to Arduino D9. Use tone() function: tone(9, 440, 500) plays note A for 0.5s. Students experiment with different frequency–duration combinations to create a custom melody of at least 5 notes. Reference: note frequency chart for C D E F G A B.
classes.materials: Passive buzzer, Arduino D9, note frequency chart, melody composition worksheet

classes.title: Build the Doorbell System in a Housing
classes.description: Mount push button on a cardboard door panel. Run wire to Arduino controller box inside the house. When button pressed: buzzer plays custom melody + LED flashes 3 times. Mount everything in housing. Present working doorbell to class.
classes.materials: Cardboard door panel + house, completed circuit, paint, wire extension, decorating materials

projects.project:
Project 9 – Plant Watering Reminder
(Soil Moisture Sensor + Visual Alert)

classes.title: Soil Moisture Sensing – Conductivity in Soil
classes.description: Review conductivity concept. Soil moisture sensor: two probes; resistance between probes changes with soil wetness. Demo: read analogRead() values with probes in dry soil, moist soil, water. Record 3 threshold zones: DRY (<300), OK (300–600), WET (>600).
classes.materials: Soil moisture sensor module, Arduino, breadboard, laptop, 3 cups (dry soil/moist soil/water), data table

classes.title: Design the Alert System – Traffic Light LEDs
classes.description: Wire 3 LEDs (red for dry, yellow for ok, green for wet) to Arduino pins 7, 8, 9. Code logic: read soil sensor → compare to thresholds → light the corresponding LED. Add a buzzer for DRY condition only (urgent alert). Test with different soil wetness.
classes.materials: 3 LEDs (R/Y/G), buzzer, resistors, breadboard, Arduino, soil moisture sensor

classes.title: Build the Plant Monitor Stand
classes.description: Mount LEDs on a small cardboard signboard next to a plant pot. Run wires to Arduino in a separate housing. Attach soil probe into soil. Demo live: soil is dry → red LED + buzzer beeps. Water plant → green LED lights. Present the smart plant gadget.
classes.materials: Cardboard signboard + housing, potted plant, completed circuit, paint, cable ties

projects.project:
Project 10 – Assistive Device Design Challenge
(Empathy + Custom Sensor Solution)

classes.title: Empathy Design – Who Do We Build For?
classes.description: Discuss assistive technology: hearing aids, screen readers, auto wheelchairs. Introduce personas: elderly person with poor vision, deaf child, person with limited hand mobility. Students choose one persona and list 3 daily challenges they face.
classes.materials: Projector, persona cards, design challenge worksheet

classes.title: Design & Prototype the Assistive Device
classes.description: Students design a sensor-based solution (e.g., vibrating wristband when phone rings for deaf users; LED notification lamp; obstacle alert buzzer). Build a working prototype using available components. Must address the persona's challenge clearly.
classes.materials: Mixed sensor kit (buzzer, LED, vibration motor, ultrasonic, PIR), Arduino, cardboard, wires

classes.title: Showcase & Evaluate – Does It Help?
classes.description: Students demonstrate the device and explain: This is for ___ because ___. When ___ happens, the device ___. Class evaluates: Does it work? Is it easy to use? Is it kind and respectful to the user? Certificates for all participants.
classes.materials: Completed devices, evaluation rubric, certificates

projects.project:
Project 1 – Smart Home Automation System
(Relays, Multiple Sensors & Control)

classes.title: Home Automation Architecture – Design First
classes.description: Introduce: relay module (Arduino controls high-voltage appliances safely). Map a smart home system: 3 zones each with a trigger (sensor) and an action (relay-controlled appliance). Students design the system map on A3 paper and list all components needed.
classes.materials: Projector, relay module demo, smart home system map worksheet

classes.title: Wire & Control a Relay with Arduino
classes.description: Students wire: 5V relay module IN pin → Arduino D3. Code: digitalWrite(3, LOW) = relay ON; HIGH = OFF. Add a PIR sensor → Arduino D2. Code: If PIR detects → relay ON (fan turns on) → wait 10s → relay OFF. Test with a small desk fan (teacher supervised).
classes.materials: 5V relay module, PIR sensor, Arduino, jumper wires, USB cable, laptop

classes.title: Add Multiple Sensors – Full System Wiring
classes.description: Expand to 3 sensor-relay pairs: (1) LDR → relay controls LED strip (auto-night light); (2) DHT11 → relay controls fan (if temp>30°C); (3) PIR → relay controls lamp. Wire all to Arduino Mega. Test each independently.
classes.materials: Arduino Mega, LDR, DHT11, PIR sensor, 3 relay modules, LED strip (5V), small fan

classes.title: Integrate & Demo the Full Smart Home
classes.description: Mount all sensors and relays into a cardboard house model (one room each). Run all 3 automations simultaneously. Demo walkthrough: walk past PIR → room light on; cover LDR → night lamp on; breathe on DHT → fan kicks in. Invite another class!
classes.materials: Completed smart home model, power supply, laptop, demo script

projects.project:
Project 2 – Automatic Fire Alarm System
(Flame Sensor + Buzzer + Multi-Zone Alert)

classes.title: Fire Detection Technology – How Detectors Work
classes.description: Types of fire detectors: smoke, heat, flame (IR). Flame sensor works on IR radiation from fire. Demo: bring a lit candle near flame sensor → output pin goes LOW. Use analogRead for analog flame intensity. Plot values at different distances.
classes.materials: Flame sensor, Arduino, candle (teacher only), serial monitor, distance-value table

classes.title: Build the Multi-Zone Alarm Circuit
classes.description: Wire 2 flame sensors (Zone A and Zone B). Each sensor triggers its own red LED + buzzer. Code: If flameA_detected → LED_A ON + beep tone; If flameB_detected → LED_B ON + different tone. Multiple zones — different sounds identify location.
classes.materials: 2 flame sensors, 2 red LEDs, buzzer, Arduino, resistors, breadboard, wires

classes.title: Build the Fire Panel & Emergency Signage
classes.description: Mount sensors in two separate cardboard rooms. Run wires to a central control panel box. Panel has 2 zone LEDs, a master reset button, and a buzzer. Add a message to LCD: FIRE IN ZONE A. Demonstrate a safe drill procedure to the class.
classes.materials: Cardboard room models + control panel box, LCD display optional, reset button, completed circuit

projects.project:
Project 3 – Servo-Powered Robotic Arm
(Servo Control + Kinematics Intro)

classes.title: Servo Motors – Precision Angular Control
classes.description: Servo vs. DC motor: servo can hold a specific angle (0–180°). Demo: Servo.write(0), Servo.write(90), Servo.write(180) — arm moves to exact positions. Introduce: PWM signal, 50Hz, 1–2ms pulse width. Students sketch a 3-joint arm diagram.
classes.materials: Servo motor, Arduino, laptop, oscilloscope or diagram, servo anatomy handout

classes.title: Build the 3-Joint Robotic Arm Frame
classes.description: Students build arm frame from cardboard/popsicle sticks: base platform (servo 1 rotates), upper arm (servo 2 lifts), forearm (servo 3 tilts), gripper (servo 4 optional). Mount servos at each joint with servo horns screwed to segments.
classes.materials: 4 servo motors (SG90), popsicle sticks/cardboard, servo horns, M2 screws, glue, base platform

classes.title: Program Arm Positions & Sequences
classes.description: Wire all servos to Arduino via PCA9685 servo driver board. Code pre-programmed positions: HOME (all 90°), REACH (arm extends), GRAB (gripper closes), LIFT (arm raises). Upload and run. Students customise 3 named positions and demonstrate.
classes.materials: PCA9685 servo driver, I2C wires, Arduino, laptop, Arm Position programming worksheet

projects.project:
Project 4 – Automated Irrigation System
(Soil Sensor + Pump + Relay)

classes.title: Smart Farming – Water Management Technology
classes.description: Video: drip irrigation systems used in Indian farms. Data: India wastes 80% of irrigation water via flood irrigation. Smart sensors reduce usage by 40%. Students map a garden irrigation system: sensors, controller, pump, pipes. Discuss: how could this help small farmers?
classes.materials: Projector, smart farming video, irrigation system diagram worksheet

classes.title: Wire the Soil Sensor, Relay & Pump
classes.description: Soil moisture sensor → Arduino A0. 5V relay → Arduino D4. Submersible water pump → relay output (powered by separate 5V rail). Code: If soil moisture < 400 → relay ON (pump runs 5 sec) → relay OFF → wait 60 sec. Wire carefully: pump and Arduino on separate power rails.
classes.materials: Soil moisture sensor, 5V relay, mini submersible pump, vinyl tubing, 2 separate 5V supplies, Arduino

classes.title: Build the Garden Model & Test Live
classes.description: Students build a model garden: 2 plant pots with probes, pump in a water reservoir, tubing going to each pot. Run the automation. Manually add water to probe cups: system detects WET → pump stays off. Remove water → soil dry → pump triggers. Demo with real plant.
classes.materials: 2 plant pots, water reservoir (tub), mini pump, tubing, completed circuit, real soil and plants

projects.project:
Project 5 – Personal Health Monitor
(Pulse Sensor + OLED Display)

classes.title: Biosensors & Healthcare Wearables
classes.description: Show wearables: Apple Watch, Fitbit, pulse oximeter. How pulse sensor works: LED shines through finger → photodiode reads light absorption that changes with heartbeat. Demo: place finger on sensor → see pulsing BPM value on serial monitor. Normal range: 60–100 BPM.
classes.materials: Pulse sensor (Pulse Sensor Amped), Arduino, laptop, serial monitor, projector

classes.title: Code the BPM Calculator & Display
classes.description: Use PulseSensor library. Configure: if pulse detected → increment beat count → calculate BPM from IBI (inter-beat interval). Print BPM to Serial Monitor and draw pulse graph in Serial Plotter. Students record their own BPM at rest vs. after 10 jumping jacks.
classes.materials: Pulse sensor, Arduino, USB cable, laptop, PulseSensor library, data recording sheet

classes.title: Add OLED Screen & Build Wearable Band
classes.description: Wire I2C OLED display (128×64 pixels). Display: BPM: XX in large font with a heart icon. Mount sensor, Arduino Nano, OLED, and battery into a fabric wristband or cardboard housing. Test as a wearable. Compare readings with a commercial pulse oximeter.
classes.materials: OLED 0.96 inch I2C, Arduino Nano, small Li-Po/9V battery, wristband (fabric or cardboard), completed sensor circuit

projects.project:
Project 6 – Python Rule-Based Chatbot
(Python Intro + If-Elif Logic)

classes.title: Introduction to Python – Your First Code
classes.description: Open Python IDLE or repl.it. Type and run: print('Hello, World!'). Introduce: variables (name = 'Asha'), string input (name = input('What is your name?')), and output. Students write a program that asks for name and age and prints a personalised greeting.
classes.materials: Computers with Python IDLE or repl.it, step-by-step worksheet, projector

classes.title: If-Elif-Else Logic – Decision Making in Python
classes.description: Teach conditional logic: if/elif/else. Build a simple quiz: 3 questions, each with if/elif/else to check the answer and print Correct! or Wrong! Count score using a variable. Print final score out of 3. Students customise with their own quiz questions.
classes.materials: Computers with Python, If-Elif-Else reference card, quiz building worksheet

classes.title: Build the Chatbot – Keyword Detection
classes.description: Teach: while True loop for continuous conversation. Dictionary of responses: if hello in user_input.lower() → print Hi there! Add 10+ keyword-response pairs (greetings, questions, jokes, quit to exit). Students add their own 5 custom responses.
classes.materials: Computers with Python, chatbot starter code file, keyword-response planning sheet

projects.project:
Project 7 – 3D Pen Holder Design
(Functional CAD + Product Thinking)

classes.title: Product Design Thinking – From Problem to Form
classes.description: Show: a messy desk with scattered pens vs. a well-organised pen holder. Discuss: user needs, aesthetics, function. Introduce product design brief: Design a pen holder for 10 pens, stable, with a compartment. Students sketch 3 concept designs.
classes.materials: Projector, desk photos, design brief handout, A4 sketch sheet

classes.title: Design the Pen Holder in Tinkercad
classes.description: Students choose best concept and model in Tinkercad: cylindrical/rectangular outer wall, hollow interior (hole to create pocket), optional small drawer (separate sliding box component). Must verify: inner diameter > 8mm so a pen fits. Apply colour/material texture.
classes.materials: Computers with Tinkercad, design brief (dimensions), Tinkercad pen holder tutorial PDF

classes.title: Review, Export & Present the Design
classes.description: Export design as STL. Students create a product one-pager: screenshot, dimensions, materials to be used, target user, price estimate. Present: My pen holder is for ___ because ___. It was designed to ___. If printer available, print at least one design.
classes.materials: Tinkercad, one-pager template, printer optional, export STL worksheet

projects.project:
Project 8 – Solar-Powered Car
(Renewable Energy + Energy Conversion)

classes.title: Solar Energy – How Photovoltaic Cells Work
classes.description: Show: solar panel powering a small motor in sunlight vs. shade. Discuss: photons → electron flow → electricity. Chart: how much solar power does India's sun provide per day? Compare to a petrol car's energy. Show the I-V curve: more light → more current.
classes.materials: Small solar panel (0.5W), DC motor with propeller, multimeter, lamp for indoor test, projector

classes.title: Build the Solar Car Chassis
classes.description: Students assemble cardboard/balsa car chassis. Mount 4 wheels on 2 axles (bamboo skewers through straws). Mount DC motor at rear with pulley-to-rear-axle belt drive. Attach solar panel on top angled toward sun. Wire: panel → motor directly (no battery).
classes.materials: Balsa/cardboard, 4 wheels, 2 skewer axles, straws, DC motor, small solar panel (0.5W+), rubber band belt

classes.title: Test, Optimise & Race in Sunlight
classes.description: Take cars to sunlight or direct lamp source. Test: does the motor spin? Speed test: measure distance in 10 seconds. Optimise: (1) reduce friction; (2) adjust panel angle; (3) reduce car weight. Race 3 rounds. Record and discuss: what affected performance most?
classes.materials: Completed solar cars, outdoor or bright lamp space, tape measure, timer, optimisation worksheet

projects.project:
Project 9 – RFID Attendance System
(RFID-RC522 + Arduino + Serial Log)

classes.title: RFID Technology – How Access Control Works
classes.description: Discuss: RFID in ID cards, library books, metro passes, toll gates. RFID-RC522 module: emits 13.56 MHz radio wave; RFID card/tag has a microchip that sends back a unique UID. Demo: scan tag → unique 8-hex UID appears on Serial Monitor.
classes.materials: RFID-RC522 module, RFID cards/fobs, Arduino, laptop, SPI wiring guide, projector

classes.title: Wire RFID & Read Card UIDs
classes.description: Wire RFID-RC522 to Arduino via SPI (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS pins). Install MFRC522 library. Upload RFID_Read example. Scan cards → read UIDs in Serial Monitor. Record each card's UID. Assign: Card A = Asha, Card B = Raj, Card C = Unknown.
classes.materials: RFID-RC522, Arduino Uno, jumper wires, laptop with MFRC522 library, UID recording sheet

classes.title: Build the Attendance Logger
classes.description: Code: match scanned UID to a name lookup. Print: WELCOME Asha - 10:45 AM or UNKNOWN CARD. Add green LED for known, red LED for unknown. Use RTC module for actual time. Build a cardboard access terminal housing. Class scans their cards!
classes.materials: RTC DS1307 module, green + red LEDs, cardboard housing, completed RFID circuit, 9V battery for standalone

projects.project:
Project 10 – Community STEM Challenge
(Design Thinking + Full Build + Pitch)

classes.title: Identify the Community Problem
classes.description: Students interview a school staff member (canteen worker, librarian, security guard) about one technology challenge they face. Write a problem statement: WHO needs WHAT because WHY? Propose 3 solution ideas using STEM concepts studied this year.
classes.materials: Interview guide sheet, problem statement template, sticky notes, projector for sharing

classes.title: Build the Technology Solution
classes.description: Students build a working prototype addressing the identified problem. Use any combination of Arduino, Python, sensors, 3D design, or mechanical systems. Focus: does it solve the real problem? Test with the staff member if possible.
classes.materials: Full mixed kit (sensors, Arduino, Python, 3D design access), build timer 35 min, checklist

classes.title: Pitch Presentation – Student as Innovator
classes.description: Students present to a panel of judges (principal, senior teachers): 2-minute pitch using: Problem, Solution demo, How it works, What they would improve. Panel Q&A (2 questions). Award Young Innovator certificates. Display in school foyer for 1 week.
classes.materials: Completed prototypes, presentation poster A3, judges rubric, certificates, display area

projects.project:
Project 1 – Bluetooth-Controlled Robot
(HC-05 + Arduino + Android App)

classes.title: Wireless Communication – Bluetooth Protocol
classes.description: Explain wireless protocols: Bluetooth (short range, 2.4GHz), Wi-Fi, Zigbee, LoRa. HC-05 Bluetooth module: AT command mode to configure baud rate and name. Demo: pair HC-05 with laptop → open Bluetooth serial terminal → type A → Arduino LED blinks.
classes.materials: HC-05 module, Arduino, laptop with Bluetooth, BT serial terminal app, AT command reference

classes.title: Wire HC-05 & Build Robot Command Logic
classes.description: Wire HC-05 RX/TX to Arduino via voltage divider (HC-05 RX is 3.3V). Connect L298N motor driver. Code: Serial.read() character: F=forward, B=back, L=left, R=right, S=stop. Test with BT terminal before connecting motors.
classes.materials: HC-05, voltage divider (1kΩ + 2kΩ), L298N, 2 motors, robot chassis, 7.4V battery, Arduino

classes.title: Control via Smartphone App
classes.description: Install Arduino Bluetooth Controller app. Configure buttons to send F/B/L/R/S characters. Pair phone → open app → drive the robot through an obstacle course! Experiment: add speed control via slider (PWM). Measure response latency.
classes.materials: Android phone with BT Controller app, completed Bluetooth robot, obstacle course, PWM speed worksheet

projects.project:
Project 2 – Ultrasonic Smart Dustbin
(Servo + Ultrasonic + Contactless Lid)

classes.title: System Design – Component Architecture
classes.description: Before building, design the system: draw block diagram (Power → Arduino → HC-SR04 → logic → Servo → lid). List all pins used. Write pseudocode. Discuss: what is hysteresis? (Detect at 15cm, close only when 30cm.) Why is this better?
classes.materials: Block diagram worksheet, pseudocode template, HC-SR04, servo for reference

classes.title: Wire & Code the Contactless Lid
classes.description: Wire HC-SR04 (Trig/Echo) + servo (signal → pin 9). Code full logic with hysteresis: If dist < 20cm for 3 consecutive readings → servo to 90° (open); If dist > 30cm for 5 readings → servo to 0° (close). Calibrate opening angle for different lid weights.
classes.materials: HC-SR04, servo SG90, Arduino, breadboard, laptop, dustbin body (plastic container)

classes.title: Build the Final Dustbin with Hidden Electronics
classes.description: Mount all electronics inside the dustbin lid. Battery pack inside. Only servo arm and HC-SR04 visible externally. Cable management: use cable ties and tape. Dustbin should look clean. Add a Smart Dustbin sticker. Demonstrate to class.
classes.materials: Plastic dustbin, completed circuit, cable ties, double-sided tape, battery pack, decorating stickers

projects.project:
Project 3 – Automated Watering Bot
(Soil Sensor + Pump + Data Logging)

classes.title: IoT in Agriculture – Smart Irrigation Data
classes.description: Discuss: traditional irrigation vs. IoT-enabled. Show: Fasal and AgroStar (Indian agri-tech startups). Sensors in field: soil moisture, temp, humidity, light. Data sent to cloud → AI decides when to water. Students design a small-scale version.
classes.materials: Projector, IoT in farming article, system design worksheet

classes.title: Multi-Sensor Wiring & Relay Control
classes.description: Wire: soil moisture sensor → A0, DHT11 temp/humidity → D2, relay → D4, pump → relay output. Code logic: If soil moisture < 300 AND temp > 25 → pump runs for 8 seconds → wait 5 minutes. Serial print all sensor values every 10 seconds.
classes.materials: Soil sensor, DHT11, relay, mini pump, vinyl tube, plant pot with soil, Arduino, laptop

classes.title: Data Logging to SD Card
classes.description: Add SD card module. Code: append log to CSV file — time, soil_moisture, temp, humidity, pump_status. Students open CSV in Excel after 10 minutes and create a line chart. Discuss: what does this data tell a farmer? How could AI use this?
classes.materials: SD card module, microSD card (2GB), SD library, laptop, Excel for chart analysis

projects.project:
Project 4 – Multi-Sensor Weather Station
(DHT22, BMP280, UV & Light Sensors)

classes.title: Professional Weather Station Design
classes.description: Show: Raspberry Pi-based weather station project. Identify all sensors needed: DHT22 (temp+humidity, more accurate), BMP280 (barometric pressure), VEML6070 (UV index), BH1750 (light lux). Students assign each sensor to a team member for research and connection.
classes.materials: Projector, sensor datasheets, weather station architecture worksheet, team assignment

classes.title: Wire & Test Each Sensor
classes.description: Wire all sensors: DHT22 → D2; BMP280 → I2C; VEML6070 → I2C; BH1750 → I2C (multiple I2C devices on same bus with different addresses). Each team member adds their sensor code to a shared sketch. Display all 5 values on Serial Monitor.
classes.materials: Arduino, DHT22, BMP280, VEML6070, BH1750, I2C bus, jumper wires, laptop with all libraries

classes.title: Build the Weather Enclosure & LCD Dashboard
classes.description: Mount all sensors in a weather-proof cardboard box with apertures. Wire I2C LCD to display: Line 1 — Temp and Humidity; Line 2 — UV and Lux; scrolling every 3 seconds. Build external mounting post. Calibrate readings vs. a reference thermometer.
classes.materials: Cardboard weatherproof box, I2C LCD, reference thermometer, completed sensor circuit

projects.project:
Project 5 – Advanced Line Follower
(IR Array + PID Control)

classes.title: PID Control Theory – Introduction
classes.description: Explain PID: Proportional (correct by amount of error), Integral (correct for accumulated error), Derivative (predict future error). Analogy: driving a car along a lane — you correct more sharply when you are further off-centre. Show PID simulation online tool.
classes.materials: Projector, PID simulation (pidtuner.com or similar), analogy worksheet

classes.title: IR Array Wiring & Sensor Calibration
classes.description: Wire 5-element IR array (TCRT5000 or QTRX) to Arduino analog pins A0–A4. Run calibration sketch: move robot back and forth over the line → record min/max values for each sensor → compute normalised position from −4 to +4.
classes.materials: 5-element IR array, Arduino, laptop, calibration sketch, black tape test strip

classes.title: Implement & Tune PID Code
classes.description: Code PID: error = position reading; P = Kp × error; D = (error − last_error)/dt; output = P+D; left_speed = base − output; right_speed = base + output. Upload and test. Start with Kp=10, Kd=5. Tune on oval track. Record best lap time.
classes.materials: PID-enabled line follower robot, oval track with curves, laptop, PID tuning log sheet, timer

projects.project:
Project 6 – Python Generative Art
(Turtle + Functions + OOP Intro)

classes.title: Python Functions – Reusable Code Blocks
classes.description: Teach: def function_name(parameters): and calling functions. Demo: def draw_square(size): uses turtle to draw a square. Call with different sizes. Introduce: loops calling functions — for i in range(8): draw_square(50+i*10). Run → expanding squares spiral!
classes.materials: Computers with Python + Turtle, function definition reference card, projector for live demo

classes.title: Generative Art Patterns – Geometry + Iteration
classes.description: Teach: turtle.pencolor(), turtle.bgcolor(), turtle.speed(). Patterns: (1) Rotating polygons: for i in range(36): draw_triangle(); turtle.right(10); (2) Colour cycling using a list of colours, change per iteration. Students experiment and create their own pattern.
classes.materials: Computers with Python Turtle, colour palette reference, My Pattern design challenge card

classes.title: Object-Oriented Art – Classes & Objects
classes.description: Intro to OOP: class Particle defines a turtle object with random colour, position, and draw() method. Create 5 Particle objects → each draws a different pattern. Run → complex multi-particle artwork emerges. Discuss: why is OOP useful in large programs?
classes.materials: Computers with Python, OOP starter code (Particle class skeleton), projector

projects.project:
Project 7 – 3D Mobile Stand Design
(CAD + Ergonomics)

classes.title: Ergonomics & Product Design Brief
classes.description: Discuss: ergonomics = designing for human comfort and health. Show data: neck strain from looking down at phone for more than 30 mins. An ergonomic stand holds the phone at eye level. Students measure sitting eye height and comfortable viewing angle. Write design spec: tilt angle, height, stability.
